_meta.notes: Orcish homeland. High desert plateau city built as nested library quadrangles — a stack of square colonnades, each smaller and quieter than the one outside it, until the innermost is the silent reading-room of the Hush-readers. Paper-clay deposits in the surrounding plateau supply most of the world's quality writing surface. Conversations indoors are conducted largely in writing.

ambientPool: The wind across the high plateau is steady, dry, and goes around the city without entering it.
Three orcs sit reading on a colonnade bench. None of them have looked up in twenty minutes.
A Cataloguer crosses the courtyard with a careful stack of slips, each holding a single word.
A Marginal hands a bound folio to a Shelver who immediately turns to the table of contents.
Even the city's stones have been chosen for their muffling acoustics.

rooms.summary: The widest of the nested squares. A colonnade ringing an open courtyard. Outsiders are received here. Speech is permitted — quietly. Most negotiations happen on benches, in murmurs.
ambientPool: An elven Vellum, in town for a paper contract, sits on a colonnade bench writing rather than speaking.
A Cataloguer accepts a bundle from a foreign messenger, weighs it, signs a receipt, and returns to their work.
A bell sounds once across the courtyard; nobody changes what they were doing.
A Marginal walks past with a folio under one arm, reading. They will reach a wall before they look up.

exits.narrative: Inward through the colonnade to the Cataloguers' quadrangle.
Outward to the paper-clay yards beyond the city.

rooms.summary: The second square, smaller and quieter. Cataloguers work at low long tables sorting slips into the orcish nested catalog system. Speech is rare; what speech happens is short.
ambientPool: A long table of slips. A Cataloguer reads three, moves them one position, satisfied.
Two Cataloguers exchange a folio with one nod.
A junior Cataloguer of nineteen, in the year before they decide whether to take the Hush-reader vow, sits at the edge and watches.
The colonnade pillars are inscribed with the catalog system's first rule, which contradicts itself in a useful way.

rooms.summary: The third square. Marginals' working ground. They write commentary in the margins of books, then commentary on commentary, then sometimes a third layer. Long-running arguments are conducted here over years.
ambientPool: A Marginal of seventy-something writes carefully into the margin of a book that has eight previous Marginals' annotations.
Two Marginals sit across a small table reading the same page, occasionally pointing.
A bound folio of pure marginalia — no original text — rests on a stand. It is its own argument.
Quill-rustle is the only sound.

exits.narrative: Inward through a low arch to the silent reading-room. You will be expected to write rather than speak.

rooms.summary: The innermost square. The silent reading-room of the Hush-readers. Speech here is not so much forbidden as impossible — the silence is structural, the air itself has been arranged to absorb sound. Communication is by marginalia.
ambientPool: A Hush-reader of forty-something turns a page with no sound.
Another Hush-reader writes a single careful word into a margin and slides the book across the table.
The first Hush-reader reads it, considers, writes a single careful word back.
The light here comes from very high windows. The air does not move.

exits.narrative: Outward through the low arch back to the Marginals' quadrangle.

rooms.summary: Outside the city walls. Paper-clay vats, drying frames, and the orcish papermakers themselves — the only orcs who work at volume and visible speed.
ambientPool: A papermaker pulls a sheet from a vat with one easy motion.
Drying frames stand in long rows; the wind across the plateau does the work.
A wagon arrives from Cataract Mile loaded with reeds for paper-fiber.
The papermakers speak loudly to each other — the only orcs you will hear at conversational volume.

exits.narrative: Back into the city, through the outer quadrangle.
